So, he's not the sharpest pencil in the box, but he does have a sensitivity to how to handle each customer. So he knows better now than to sort of surprise me when I come in, because the Taylor Shoppers and the dry cleaners are owned by the same guy in this small town, which is very smart. Because he gets me out of there every month or more often with cleaning. But he's learned not to try and move me six steps to look at the new stuff when I'm in there for that, because I'm doing it five minutes before I'm meeting somebody for dinner, and so he now calls. So you're coming in to pick up cleaning. We got cleaning here for you. When are you coming in? Could you allow an extra 15 minutes, because the new fabric swatches are in and there's a couple things I know you're going to want. So that's permission to sell, right? And now he's got permission to start this process, that he's got a whole bunch of benchmarks he's got to get through all the way to you trying something on. So permission to sell is something clumsy, awkward salespeople don't understand. It's also where marketing fits sort of seamlessly, because marketing leading up to the selling can get the permission in advance for that person to do his thing. So he doesn't have to be particularly sophisticated and adept at it. The marketing has done it for him. So permission to sell is number one. Number two is the offer. We make a lot of offers at GKS. Your sales folks are calling up people with offers all the time. Just discuss that a little bit. And again, we're going to get into more depth later on. You know, I think what it has to be, it has to be clear and it has to be concise. You know, at the end of the day, people enjoy to buy, but they have to be told how to buy. And that's really where the offer comes into place. A confused mind won't buy. So you put everything on the table in front of somebody, your catalog, so to speak, and say, you know, which one you want, or throwing the marbles on the table, so to speak. No one is really going to be purchased. So it needs to be clear and concise. Okay. There's more of the one place for the offer, by the way. So again, to his consultative sale model, the diagnostic call, whatever it is called, or the diagnostic appointment in healthcare, the actual exam and diagnosis, that's an offer. And it should be structured like an offer. So like, we have things for diagnostic processes of the seven things you will know, the seven questions that will be answered by this diagnostic call. So there's a benefit part of that offer. The onset is, even if we don't go any further, and you don't want to do business with me, as a result of the call alone, you're getting some value because you will know the answer to these questions. So there's that offer, all the way to the offer of, here, it's six cups in a box, and you can have your logo on them, and you get saucers for free if you do it by 10 o'clock tomorrow morning. Right, got it. We're going to just skip over the next one, which is presentation, because we're going to discuss presentation. Dan, emotional logic, talk about that, because that's a really cool, which I don't think a lot of people are aware of. It is, to the best of my knowledge, a zig-ziggler lingo, and it refers to the blending of facts, figures, statistics, rational reasons to buy, which most marketers, copywriters, and sales professionals put too much weight on, or get too soon. So they are telling you facts about single stitching versus double stitching, and before you even have decided you want to suit. So there's either too much weight put on it, or it gets too, too quickly. And some people rely too much on emotion, and don't have then any rational basis for which a purchase can be made. And there's a place in the middle of that where it blends, and zig called it emotional logic. Yeah, beautiful. Closing the sale, we're going to cover closing the sale in tremendous depth.
